{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.7, "R1.5": 1.0, "R1.6": 0.9}}

Reorganized the section into a clean motivation → contribution → method → training → results flow, removed three near-duplicate restatements of the "ambiguous queries are hard" point, and tightened claims to what the evidence supports.

Key issues in the original:
1. The motivation appears three times in nearly identical form (paragraphs 3, 5, and 6). Collapse to one statement.
2. Order is inverted: results land before the reader knows what the system is or why it matters. Standard order is motivation → contribution → method → setup → results.
3. "Outperforms strong retrieval baselines by 14 F1 points" is reported without naming the baselines or the comparison condition; "0.71 F1 overall" is on an unspecified slice. Either name the comparison or qualify.
4. "Validates the joint objective" overclaims from a single-slice delta — soften to "is consistent with" or report an ablation.
5. "First end-to-end model that jointly learns to ask clarification questions and to rerank" is a strong novelty claim and belongs near the top, not as a closing aside.

Revised version:

---

A central difficulty in modern information retrieval is that user queries are frequently ambiguous: identical surface forms can map to several distinct user intents, and existing retrievers do not handle this multi-intent case robustly. Even strong neural rerankers struggle to commit to a single interpretation and tend to return blended or partially relevant results.

We introduce AmbiQA, which, to our knowledge, is the first end-to-end model that jointly learns to ask clarification questions and to rerank retrieval candidates under a single training objective. AmbiQA is a dual-head transformer that, conditioned on the user's query and the top-k candidates returned by a BM25 first-stage retriever, simultaneously emits (i) a clarification question and (ii) a passage reranking distribution. The reranker is trained with a contrastive loss against the gold passage; the clarification head is trained with a span-prediction loss against annotated clarification turns from prior work. At inference time, a gating classifier (§2.3) decides whether to surface a clarification turn to the user or to proceed directly with reranking.

We train on 48k query–clarification pairs from ClariQ, augmented with 12k synthetic pairs generated by prompting an instruction-tuned LM with seed queries. We evaluate on three benchmarks — TREC-CAR, AmbigQA, and ClariQ. On the disambiguation slice of AmbigQA, AmbiQA improves over [named baselines] by 14 F1 points and reaches 0.71 F1 overall, a gain consistent with the joint clarification-and-reranking objective; we report ablations isolating each head in §4.

---

Uncertainty: I assumed the baselines and the precise meaning of "0.71 F1 overall" (overall AmbigQA vs. overall across the three benchmarks) — fill in the bracketed baseline names and confirm the scope of the 0.71 figure. The "first end-to-end" claim is preserved as written; verify against recent joint-clarification-reranking work (e.g., MIMICS-derived systems, ConvAI clarification rerankers) before camera-ready.
